identity.name: LSASS Memory Credential Dump via MiniDumpWriteDump (T1003.001)
identity.summary: An attacker with local administrator rights opens a handle to lsass.exe and writes its memory to a dump file (often via comsvcs.dll or a custom binary). The dump is then parsed offline with Mimikatz or pypykatz to recover NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and cleartext credentials cached in LSASS.
identity.description: LSASS credential theft remains the highest-value Windows post-exploitation primitive. In the Unit 42 'BlackSuit Blitz' incident, Ignoble Scorpius compromised domain controllers and extracted password hashes as the pivot from initial access to enterprise-wide encryption. Modern variants evade legacy AV by using legitimate Windows utilities (comsvcs.dll → MiniDump), direct syscalls, or PPL bypass. Cortex XDR's prevention stack (Behavioral Threat Protection + Credential Theft Module) is the canonical POV demonstration on this technique.

execution.framework_reference_id: T1003.001 — Atomic Test #1 (Dump LSASS.exe Memory using ProcDump) / Atomic Test #3 (Dump LSASS using comsvcs.dll)

prerequisites.description: Local administrator rights on the target Windows endpoint.
Cortex XDR agent installed and reporting healthy.

payload.code:
# Atomic Test variant — dump LSASS via comsvcs.dll MiniDump export (lives-off-the-land).
$lsassPid = (Get-Process lsass).Id
$dumpPath = "$env:TEMP\lsass-${lsassPid}.dmp"
rundll32.exe C:\Windows\System32\comsvcs.dll MiniDump $lsassPid $dumpPath full
Get-Item $dumpPath | Select-Object FullName, Length

expected_artifacts.description: rundll32.exe spawned with comsvcs.dll MiniDump arguments.
expected_artifacts.expected_value: process_name=rundll32.exe AND command_line CONTAINS 'comsvcs.dll' AND command_line CONTAINS 'MiniDump'

expected_artifacts.description: Process access to lsass.exe with PROCESS_VM_READ or PROCESS_QUERY_INFORMATION right.
Dump file written to disk (>50MB typical).
comsvcs.dll loaded by rundll32 outside normal scheduled-task pattern.

iocs.context: Memory dump file typically created in writable temp directory.

biocs.name: LSASS Handle Open With Sensitive Access Rights
biocs.description: Any process (excluding allowlisted agents) opens a handle to lsass.exe with PROCESS_VM_READ (0x0010) or PROCESS_DUP_HANDLE (0x0040) access rights.
biocs.logic:
preset = xdr_data
| filter event_type = ENUM.PROCESS
| filter event_sub_type = ENUM.PROCESS_ACCESS
| filter target_process_name = "lsass.exe"
| filter requested_access bitand 0x0410 != 0
| filter actor_process_image_name not in ("MsMpEng.exe", "cyserver.exe", "WmiPrvSE.exe")

biocs.name: comsvcs.dll MiniDump LOLBin Execution
biocs.description: rundll32.exe invokes comsvcs.dll MiniDump export — a well-known living-off-the-land credential dump vector.
biocs.logic:
preset = xdr_data
| filter event_type = ENUM.PROCESS
| filter event_sub_type = ENUM.PROCESS_START
| filter actor_process_image_name = "rundll32.exe"
| filter action_process_image_command_line contains_any ("comsvcs.dll MiniDump", "comsvcs.dll, MiniDump", "comsvcs MiniDump")

xql_queries.name: Confirm LSASS access from non-allowlisted process
xql_queries.query:
preset = xdr_data
| filter _time > to_timestamp(current_time() - 1800)
| filter event_type = ENUM.PROCESS and event_sub_type = ENUM.PROCESS_ACCESS
| filter target_process_name = "lsass.exe"
| filter requested_access bitand 0x0410 != 0
| fields _time, agent_hostname, actor_process_image_name, actor_process_command_line, requested_access

xql_queries.name: Confirm comsvcs.dll MiniDump invocation
xql_queries.query:
preset = xdr_data
| filter _time > to_timestamp(current_time() - 1800)
| filter event_type = ENUM.PROCESS and event_sub_type = ENUM.PROCESS_START
| filter action_process_image_command_line contains "comsvcs.dll" and action_process_image_command_line contains "MiniDump"
| fields _time, agent_hostname, action_process_image_command_line, causality_actor_process_image_name

correlation_rules.name: Credential Theft — LSASS Memory Dump
correlation_rules.logic: BIOC(LSASS Handle Open With Sensitive Access Rights) OR BIOC(comsvcs.dll MiniDump LOLBin Execution)

analytics_modules: Cortex XDR Credential Theft Protection Module
Cortex XDR Behavioral Threat Protection (BTP) — Credential Theft cluster

products.evidence_query: Playbook: Credential Theft Response — isolate host, force domain-wide password reset for impacted accounts, ticket DFIR.
WildFire verdict on any dropped Mimikatz binary downstream of the dump.

use_cases.name: Ransomware Kill-Chain Disruption — Credential Theft Stage
use_cases.description: Block or detect LSASS credential dumping, the pivot stage of nearly every ransomware kill chain (BlackSuit Blitz, DragonForce, etc.).

test_cases.objective: Cortex XDR Credential Theft Protection blocks the comsvcs.dll MiniDump attempt before the dump file is fully written.
preconditions: Cortex XDR agent >= 8.4.0 installed on target.
Profile includes Credential Theft Protection enabled in Block mode.
steps: Confirm rundll32 process is terminated and dump file is absent or zero-byte.
success_criteria: Process terminated within 5s; XDR alert `BTP-CRED-LSASS-001` raised with severity High.
Dump file size = 0 bytes or absent.
End-user notified via agent toast (if enabled).

test_cases.objective: If prevention is in Report-Only mode, BIOC + correlation rule still fire as detection.
success_criteria: BIOC `LSASS Handle Open With Sensitive Access Rights` fires within 30s.
Cortex XDR Endpoint Isolation applied to host within 90s.
Investigation ticket created in SOAR + ITSM.

preventive_controls: Enable Cortex XDR Credential Theft Protection in Block mode for all production endpoints.
Enable LSA Protection (RunAsPPL) on supported Windows builds.
Restrict local administrator membership; implement LAPS for the local admin password.
detection_engineering: Tune BIOC allowlist for legitimate processes that access LSASS (AV, EDR, backup agents) — alert on everything else.
Build hunts for comsvcs.dll MiniDump, rundll32.exe with unusual exports, and Sysmon Event ID 10 with GrantedAccess 0x1010 or 0x1410.
Correlate LSASS access events with downstream Mimikatz indicators (kerberos ticket extraction, DC sync calls).

references.title: Anatomy of an Attack: The 'BlackSuit Blitz' at a Global Equipment Manufacturer
